a: Data integration is the work of pulling data out of many separate systems and combining it into one place so it can be used together. The goal is one unified, consistent view instead of facts scattered across tools.
fs: Data integration moves and combines data from many sources into a single trusted view people can query.
fj: Thinking it just means copying files around. The hard part is making mismatched data line up and agree.

a: A data source is any place your data comes from, like a database, a SaaS app such as Salesforce, an API, or a CSV file. In integration work, you connect to each source to read its data.
fs: A data source is any origin system you read from; integration starts by listing and connecting to each one.
fj: Assuming there is only one source. Real projects pull from many sources that each behave differently.

a: ETL stands for Extract, Transform, Load. You extract data from a source, transform it into a clean and consistent shape, then load it into a destination like a data warehouse.
fs: ETL is the three-step pattern (extract, transform, load) that moves and reshapes data into a destination.
fj: Confusing ETL with ELT. In ELT you load raw first, then transform inside the warehouse; the order differs.

a: A data pipeline is an automated path that carries data from a source to a destination, running the steps in order. Once built, it runs on a schedule so fresh data keeps flowing without manual work.
fs: A pipeline is the automated, scheduled flow that moves data through ordered steps from source to destination.
fj: Picturing a one-time manual export. A pipeline is repeatable and runs on its own, again and again.

a: Schema mapping is matching fields from a source to fields in the destination, even when the names or formats differ. For example, the source's "cust_id" maps to the warehouse's "customer_id".
fs: Schema mapping defines how each source field lines up with a destination field so data lands correctly.
fj: Expecting field names to always match. Sources use different names and types, so you must map them on purpose.
